description: This chart illustrates the relationship between alloy density (unit: g/cm³) and aluminum content (unit: wt.-%, ranging from 5 wt.% to 13 wt.%) for ferrous alloys. Triangular markers represent experimental density data of binary Al-Fe alloys compiled by Frommeyer and Drewes (2000), square markers represent experimental density data of quaternary 0.2C-8.5Mn alloys measured via picnometry in this work, and the continuous line corresponds to density calculation results of the quaternary system obtained using Thermo-Calc software. The overall trend shows that alloy density decreases almost linearly with increasing Al addition: density drops from ~7.3 g/cm³ at 5 wt.% Al to ~6.5 g/cm³ at 13 wt.% Al, and the quaternary 0.2C-8.5Mn alloys exhibit slightly lower density than binary Al-Fe alloys at the same Al content. This figure supports the core conclusion that Al addition is an effective approach to reduce steel density, which is a critical design parameter for low-density automotive lightweight steels, and the Thermo-Calc calculation shows good consistency with experimental density data.

This set of three isoplethal phase diagrams are calculated for quaternary 8Mn-0.2C (wt.%) low-density steel, with x-axis representing Al content ranging from 0 to 10 wt.% and y-axis representing temperature ranging from 600 to 1400 ℃. Subplot (a) adopts the PrecHiMn-01 database incorporating Chin et al.'s assessment, showing extra low-temperature phase regions including cementite and M5C2 carbide besides common phases (FCC austenite, BCC ferrite, liquid, κ phase). Subplot (b) adopts the PrecHiMn-02 database incorporating Umino et al.'s assessment, with no low-temperature carbide phase regions labeled. Subplot (c) adopts the PrecHiMn-03 database incorporating Lindahl and Selleby's assessment, presenting optimized low-temperature κ phase prediction. The comparison demonstrates that different thermodynamic assessment databases lead to distinct phase stability prediction results, which provides important guidance for CALPHAD modeling and microstructure regulation of Al-containing low-density automotive steels.

This chart plots the calculated fraction of FCC (austenite) phase as a function of temperature ranging from 600 °C to 1400 °C for 8Mn-0.2C (wt.%) low-density steels with 6 wt.% Al and 8 wt.% Al additions, using three different CALPHAD thermodynamic databases (PrecHiMn-01, PrecHiMn-02, PrecHiMn-03). All curves show a unimodal trend: FCC fraction increases with temperature first, reaches a peak at ~900–1000 °C, then decreases at higher temperatures. For the same database, 6 wt.% Al alloy exhibits higher maximum FCC fraction than 8 wt.% Al alloy, as Al is a strong ferrite stabilizer. Obvious discrepancies exist between database results: PrecHiMn-02 gives the highest FCC fraction (peak ~0.75), while PrecHiMn-03 gives the lowest values. This figure demonstrates that current thermodynamic databases for Fe-Al-Mn-C system show non-negligible deviations, requiring further experimental validation to support low-density steel composition and process design.

This is an isothermal section phase diagram of the Fe-rich corner in the Fe-Mn-Al-C quaternary system at 850 °C with fixed Al content of 8 wt.%. The horizontal axis represents Mn content ranging from 0 to 25 wt.%, and the vertical axis represents C content ranging from 0 to 1.0 wt.%. Distinct phase fields are clearly labeled, including single bcc phase, bcc + κ phase, bcc + fcc + κ three-phase, bcc + fcc dual-phase, fcc + κ dual-phase, and single fcc phase regions. Key trends are observed: bcc phase is stable at low Mn and low C contents, increasing Mn and C contents stabilizes the fcc phase, while higher C content promotes κ carbide precipitation, and single fcc phase is stabilized at sufficiently high Mn and C levels. This diagram guides the composition design of three families of low-density steels (ferritic, dual-phase, austenitic) for automotive lightweighting applications, supporting the conclusion that phase constituents can be tailored by adjusting C and Mn contents at fixed 8 wt.% Al and 850 °C processing temperature.

This set of micrographs presents typical microstructures of three families of high-Al low-density steels for automotive lightweighting applications. Subfigure (a) shows the single-phase equiaxed ferrite grain microstructure of binary Fe-Al ferritic high-Al steel with a 40 μm scale bar. Subfigure (b) is an electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) map of dual-phase austenite-ferrite high-Al steel (added C and Mn to stabilize austenite) with a 50 μm scale bar, where austenite is marked in red and distributed as band-like structures along the rolling direction, and ferrite appears gray. Subfigure (c) is an EBSD map of three-phase TRIPLEX austenitic high-Al steel (with high C and Mn contents) with a 20 μm scale bar, where small amounts of ferrite distributed at grain boundaries are marked in blue. Nanoscale κ phase is not observed in all maps due to its extremely small size. This figure verifies the classification of three high-Al steel families based on microstructural constituents, supporting the microstructure regulation strategy for developing high-performance lightweight automotive steels.

This plot is a strain rate-temperature map for Fe-8 wt.% Al ferritic low-density steel, illustrating the activation domain of discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) during hot deformation. The x-axis represents temperature ranging from 850 °C to 1150 °C, while the y-axis is a log-scale strain rate from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 100 s⁻¹. Green dots mark conditions where DDRX is not activated, red dots mark conditions where DDRX is activated, and the black dashed line denotes the boundary between the two domains. Data are derived from hot torsion tests performed at 900–1100 °C with strain rates of 0.1–10 s⁻¹. The map demonstrates that DDRX is activated at higher temperature and higher strain rate conditions above the boundary, while conditions below the boundary (lower temperature, lower strain rate) do not activate DDRX, where continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) occurs instead. This figure clarifies dynamic recrystallization rules for the ferritic low-density steel, providing reference for its hot processing parameter optimization.

This set of micrographs illustrates κ-phase microstructures in 2Mn-8Al-0.2C (wt.%) low-density automotive steel. Subfigure (a) is an optical micrograph (20 μm scale bar) of the sample cooled from 1400 °C at 10 °C/s and quenched at 870 °C, showing κ-phase precipitated at the interface of austenite (γ) grains embedded in ferrite (α) matrix. Subfigure (b) is a 1.46 kX SEM micrograph (10 μm scale bar) of the same sample after deep etching, revealing the 3D morphology of needle-like κ-phase growing along the long axis of austenite. Subfigure (c) is a 2.00 kX SEM micrograph (10 μm scale bar) of the sample isothermally held at 870 °C for 15 min followed by deep etching, showing cocoon-shaped κ-phase remaining after full austenite decomposition. The figure confirms κ-phase preferentially precipitates at α/γ interfaces in low-Mn low-density steels, with distinct morphologies under different heat treatments, supporting the non-lamellar eutectoid decomposition mechanism of this alloy system.

This figure presents TEM characterization of microstructure evolution for 28.8Mn-9.4Al-0.92C (wt.%) low-density austenitic steel during aging at 500 °C. Four subfigures (a) to (d) correspond to different aging durations: (a) initial state plus 2 min aging, (b) 16 min aging, (c) 4 h aging, (d) 64 h aging. Each subfigure includes a selected area electron diffraction pattern of austenite along [100] zone axis, and a dark-field TEM image acquired using {100} superlattice reflections. The κ phase precipitation evolves with aging time: weak diffuse superlattice reflections appear at initial stage, become more intense after 16 min aging, round κ precipitates smaller than 5 nm are clearly observed after 4 h aging, and cuboidal κ precipitates of 5-10 nm arranged along <100> directions of austenite form after 64 h aging. This figure demonstrates the nucleation, growth, morphology and orientation evolution of κ phase during aging, supporting the precipitation regulation mechanism for low-density automotive steels.

This figure presents TEM characterization of ferrite microstructure evolution in 29Mn-9.6Al-0.56C (wt.%) low-density automotive steel during aging at 550 °C. It contains four subgraphs: the first is the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of as-quenched ferrite collected along the [011]α zone axis, the second is the corresponding dark-field TEM image acquired using {200} reflection with a 100 nm scale bar; the third is the SAED pattern of ferrite after 1 h aging at 550 °C, the fourth is its matching dark-field TEM image with a 100 nm scale bar. The as-quenched SAED shows clear intensity difference between {111} and {200} reflections, confirming a mixed B2 and DO3 ordered structure. After aging, the intensity of these two reflections becomes comparable, indicating ferrite transforms to a fully ordered DO3 structure. Dark-field images also show ordered precipitates coarsen after aging. This figure reveals the ordering evolution rule of ferrite in high Al low-density steels during thermal aging.

This plot demonstrates the tensile strength (TS, in MPa) of 8.5Mn-0.2C (wt.%) austenite-ferrite low-density steels as a function of annealing temperature (T, in °C, ranging from 800 °C to 1000 °C), with variables including alloy composition (6 wt.% Al, 6 wt.% Al with extra Si, 7 wt.% Al) and annealing duration (1 min, 136 sec, 5 min). Green curves correspond to 6Al alloys, red curves to 6Al+XSi alloys, and blue curves to 7Al alloys, with different markers for different annealing times. For all alloy groups, TS consistently decreases with increasing annealing temperature. At the same annealing temperature and duration, 6Al+XSi alloys show the highest TS, followed by 7Al alloys, while 6Al alloys have the lowest TS. For each composition, longer annealing time leads to lower TS. This figure supports that annealing parameters (temperature, holding time) and alloying (Al content, Si addition) can be tailored to adjust the tensile strength of such low-density steels for automotive lightweighting applications.

This set of 4 electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) maps characterize the microstructure of 8.5Mn-6Al-XSi-0.2C (wt.%) low-density automotive steel annealed for 136 s at two temperatures: 800°C and 1000°C. Subfigure (a) shows the band contrast (BC) map of the 800°C-annealed sample with austenite marked in red, while (c) presents the BC + inverse pole figure (IPF) map of ferrite for the same sample. Subfigure (b) shows the BC map of the 1000°C-annealed sample with austenite marked in red, while (d) presents the BC + IPF map of ferrite for the 1000°C sample. At 800°C, ferrite grains are pancake-shaped and incompletely recrystallized, with fine band-like distributed austenite. At 1000°C, both ferrite and austenite grains are coarser, ferrite recrystallization is complete, and grain orientation variation is reduced compared to the lower annealing temperature. This figure supports that higher annealing temperature promotes full recrystallization of ferrite in the dual-phase low-density steel, altering phase distribution and grain orientation characteristics.

This figure presents engineering stress-strain curves of a fully austenitic 0.92C-28.8Mn-9.4Al (wt.%) low-density steel for automotive applications, with 6 samples of different austenite grain sizes (1.65 μm, 2.2 μm, 8.4 μm, 12.4 μm, 21 μm, 40 μm) tested. The x-axis represents elongation in %, and the y-axis represents engineering stress in MPa. It is observed that smaller grain size leads to higher yield strength and ultimate tensile strength, but lower elongation: the 1.65 μm grain sample reaches a tensile strength close to 1000 MPa with ~47% total elongation, while the 40 μm grain sample shows ~800 MPa tensile strength and ~61% total elongation. Elongation shows a saturation effect when grain size exceeds 10 μm. The results validate that the mechanical properties of this alloy follow the Hall-Petch relation, demonstrating that grain size adjustment is an effective approach to tune the strength-formability balance of this low-density steel for automotive lightweighting.

This figure displays a semi-transparent schematic of a passenger vehicle body-in-white frame, with two blue highlighted regions marking the front energy-absorbing structural components used for crash performance assessment. This schematic corresponds to the finite element crash simulation model described in the work, which is used to compare the crashworthiness of high-aluminum low-density steels against conventional DP600 automotive steel. Simulations demonstrate that dual-phase low-density steel delivers 21% better crash performance, and austenitic low-density steel delivers 21-33% better performance across frontal crash and A-pillar intrusion test scenarios, validating that low-density steels with 8-10% lower density than conventional steels are suitable for automotive lightweighting applications while meeting mechanical safety requirements.

This schematic diagram displays the transparent body-in-white structure of a passenger car, with two key load-bearing structural components highlighted in red, which represent the preferred application positions of low-density high-aluminum steels for automotive lightweighting as discussed in the paper. The investigated low-density steels, including ferritic, ferrite-austenite dual-phase, and austenitic steels with nanoscale κ-phase precipitates, have 8-10% lower density than conventional low-carbon steels, while achieving excellent mechanical properties: dual-phase low-density steel has 646 MPa yield strength, 800 MPa tensile strength and 26% elongation, while austenitic low-density steel has 812 MPa yield strength, 1027 MPa tensile strength and 32% elongation. Crash simulation results show these steels have 21-33% better crash performance than reference DP600 steel, supporting their application potential in automotive structures.
